metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the trigger mechanism in the spec itself (pick systemd timer OR GitHub Actions SSM, state the polling cadence / expected latency bound, and justify the choice) rather than deferring 'choose ONE' to the implementor.
findings.finding: The trigger mechanism (systemd timer vs GitHub Actions SSM) is explicitly left as a choice for the executor ('choose ONE and justify in the plan'), making it a placeholder design decision rather than a resolved spec. An executing agent must stop and decide this architecture question before proceeding.
findings.location: What section — Trigger bullet; AC 5 ('Chosen trigger mechanism + cadence recorded')
findings.suggested_fix: Select one: e.g. 'Use a systemd timer on the box polling origin/main every 5 minutes; expected p95 deploy latency ≤ 10 min after merge.' Document the rationale (e.g. no outbound internet dependency, simpler IAM surface) and remove the (a)/(b) fork from the spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Choose the trigger mechanism in the plan and state a positive rationale: the systemd-timer option (option a) aligns directly with the existing `install-observability.sh` and `install-certbot-timer.sh` patterns already proven on this box, requires no new IAM, and keeps the deploy loop entirely on-box; OR if GitHub Actions (option b) is selected, document the required `ssm:SendCommand` IAM grant on the instance role (absent from `infra/terraform/iam.tf`) and the AWS credential secret needed in GitHub Actions.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly defers the trigger mechanism choice ('choose ONE and justify in the plan') without making the choice, leaving the implementer with an unresolved design decision and providing no positive rationale for the selected approach.
findings.location: What section — 'Trigger' bullet; AC line 'Chosen trigger mechanism + cadence recorded'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt option (a) — systemd timer polling `origin/main` every 5 minutes — to match the existing pattern in `infra/scripts/install-observability.sh` and `infra/scripts/install-certbot-timer.sh`. Add a new `install-autodeploy.sh` alongside those scripts, model the unit file on `rebar-observability.{service,timer}`, and record the 5-minute polling cadence + ≤5-minute median latency in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full lifecycle of the deployed-SHA state: initial value/seed (first run / post-manual-deploy), where it is persisted (e.g., a `.deploy-sha` file), that it is written atomically (write-to-tmp then rename) only after a successful deploy, and a RETIRE path (cleanup on disable/uninstall).
findings.finding: The 'deployed SHA' — the key shared state driving all change-detection decisions — has no defined CREATE lifecycle (what initializes it on first run or after a manual deploy?), no atomic UPDATE path (the script reads the SHA, compares, acts, then writes the new SHA, which is a classic check-then-act TOCTOU), and no RETIRE/cleanup path.
findings.location: What section — 'Container redeploy (change-detected)' + AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Persist the deployed SHA in a file (e.g., `/var/lib/gerrit-autodeploy/deployed.sha`). On first run (file absent), treat the current `docker inspect` label or `git rev-parse HEAD` of the deploy checkout as the baseline. Write the new SHA only after the health-check passes, using `echo $NEW_SHA > $FILE.tmp && mv $FILE.tmp $FILE` for atomicity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the health-check protocol (what endpoint/command is probed, pass/fail criteria, timeout/retry count) and co-locate with the rollback section a step-by-step unwinding rule for each partial-update state (config-only applied, image-built-not-started, container-started-but-unhealthy).
findings.finding: The rollback protocol references 'roll back the container to the prior image; keep the prior config .bak' but the state machine for WHAT constitutes a 'failed deploy' (i.e., what the health check tests, what its pass/fail signal is, and how partial-update state is detected and unwound) is never specified — the decision rule is referenced but not co-located with any schema.
findings.location: What section / Safety bullet / AC line 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If trigger option (b) is chosen, specify the AWS identity the GitHub Actions workflow uses: declare an OIDC IdP (`aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com`), an assumed-role ARN, and an IAM policy that scopes `ssm:SendCommand` to the specific Gerrit instance ID (not `*`) — so no long-lived key is stored in GitHub Actions secrets and the grant is least-privileged.
findings.finding: If trigger option (b) is chosen — GitHub Actions `deploy.yml` invoking the deploy script via `ssm:SendCommand` — the GitHub Actions runner becomes a lower-trust actor that must hold an AWS identity capable of executing commands on the Gerrit EC2 instance. The plan names this path but specifies nothing about that identity: no OIDC provider (`aws_iam_openid_connect_provider`), no assumed-role ARN, no scoped IAM policy for `ssm:SendCommand`. The existing `iam*.tf` files contain no such grant. Left unspecified, this is typically filled in with a long-lived AWS access key stored as a GitHub Actions secret (a secret-on-boundary-crossing-path violation) or an overly-broad assumed role — both are least-privilege failures on a path where a compromise yields RCE on the host.
findings.location: What > Trigger section: 'a GitHub Actions `deploy.yml` on `push: main` that invokes the deploy script via SSM'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` and an `aws_iam_role` with a trust policy conditioned on `repo:navapbc/rebar:ref:refs/heads/main` (token.actions.githubusercontent.com sub). Scope the role's inline policy to `ssm:SendCommand` on `arn:aws:ec2:REGION:ACCOUNT:instance/i-00880b2c7f13527c5` and `ssm:GetCommandInvocation` on `*`. Reference this role ARN from the `deploy.yml` via `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` with `role-to-assume`. Add a corresponding `aws_iam_role_policy "rebar-gerrit-gha-deploy"` in `iam_s2.tf` or a new `iam_autodeploy.tf`, matching the single-owner IAM pattern in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If trigger option (b) (GitHub Actions → SSM RunCommand) is chosen, verify and scope the IAM prerequisite: a GitHub OIDC trust + scoped `ssm:SendCommand` IAM role is needed and must be added to the plan's scope (or explicitly ruled out to preserve the ADR-0008 'no static keys' invariant).
findings.finding: The plan's option (b) — 'a GitHub Actions deploy.yml on push: main that invokes the deploy script via SSM' — asserts this is a viable trigger without verifying the IAM prerequisites. The GitHub Actions runner would need AWS credentials with `ssm:SendCommand` (and `ec2:DescribeInstances` for targeting) on the production instance. The existing IAM model (EC2 instance role for box-side SSM, scoped to `/rebar/prod/*` SSM params) does not cover an inbound GitHub→AWS push credential, and this has not been provisioned or documented.
findings.location: What section — 'Trigger' option (b)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) choose the systemd-timer option (b) which needs no new IAM surface and matches the existing observability pattern, or (b) if GitHub Actions dispatch is preferred, add an explicit IAM sub-task: create a GitHub OIDC identity provider + IAM role with `ssm:SendCommand` scoped to the single instance, consistent with ADR-0008's 'no static AWS keys'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If option (b) is chosen, specify the AWS identity the GitHub Actions deploy workflow uses — at minimum: an OIDC federated role (no long-lived keys), scoped to `ssm:SendCommand` on the specific Gerrit instance ARN (`arn:aws:ec2:…:instance/i-00880b2c7f13527c5`), with the role and OIDC provider declared in a new `iam_autodeploy.tf`; record this in the plan before the trigger choice is finalised.
findings.finding: If the chosen trigger is option (b) — GitHub Actions `deploy.yml` invoking the deploy script via SSM — the GitHub Actions runner needs an AWS identity with at minimum `ssm:SendCommand` on the Gerrit instance. The plan names this trigger option but specifies nothing about the AWS identity the workflow would use (no OIDC provider, no assumed-role ARN, no scoped IAM policy). The existing iam*.tf files contain no `ssm:SendCommand` grant and no OIDC IdP resource. Because the trigger choice is explicitly deferred ('choose ONE and justify in the plan'), one of the two named options carries a completely unspecified IAM contract — a deploy credential that could end up as a long-lived key or an overly-broad role if filled in without explicit guidance.
findings.location: What > Trigger section: 'choose ONE and justify in the plan — (a) … or (b) a GitHub Actions `deploy.yml` on `push: main` that invokes the deploy script via SSM'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` for `token.actions.githubusercontent.com` (if not already present) and an `aws_iam_role` with a trust policy scoped to the specific repository + `push: main` ref condition, with a single inline policy granting only `ssm:SendCommand` on `arn:aws:ssm:${region}:${account}:*` and `ssm:GetCommandInvocation` — then attach it as `role-to-assume` in the workflow via the `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` action (OIDC, no stored key).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] If trigger option (b) is selected, state the auth precondition explicitly: a GitHub Actions IAM role with `ssm:SendCommand` + OIDC federation must be provisioned (new Terraform), or alternatively note that option (a) (systemd timer on the box) avoids this entirely since the box already has the instance role.
findings.finding: If trigger option (b) — 'GitHub Actions deploy.yml … via SSM' — is chosen, no IAM role or OIDC provider granting GitHub Actions `ssm:SendCommand` on the target instance exists in the current Terraform. The existing IAM setup is an EC2 instance role only; there is no GitHub Actions → AWS OIDC federation configured.
findings.location: Trigger section — option (b) 'a GitHub Actions deploy.yml on push: main that invokes the deploy script via SSM'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the prior image is preserved before each deploy (e.g., `docker tag` to a stable rollback tag), confirm it survives any image-prune automation, and define the `.bak` file naming convention and a maximum retention count (e.g., keep last 2).
findings.finding: The rollback plan references restoring 'the container to the prior image', but neither the identity/tag of that prior image nor a mechanism to prevent it from being garbage-collected (e.g., `docker image prune`) is specified. Similarly, config `.bak` files are mentioned but their creation, naming, and cleanup are left undefined.
findings.location: What section — 'Safety': 'roll back the container to the prior image; keep the prior config .bak'
findings.suggested_fix: Before `docker compose up --build`, run `docker tag gerrit-autodeploy-review-bot:latest gerrit-autodeploy-review-bot:rollback` to pin the current image. In the rollback step, `docker tag :rollback :latest && docker compose up -d review-bot`. Limit `.bak` files to 3 copies with a `find ... -name '*.bak' | sort | head -n -3 | xargs rm -f`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or define the canonical on-box repo checkout path (e.g. /opt/rebar) that the auto-deploy mechanism will use; no existing script or runbook establishes this path, so it must be explicitly specified and bootstrapped as part of this plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the existence of 'the box's deploy checkout (the compose build context)' as a known, locatable entity, but no script, runbook, or infrastructure file establishes, names, or documents a persistent repo checkout path on the EC2 box. compose-up.sh derives REPO_ROOT dynamically from the script's own location, but the canonical on-box path for the checkout is undefined in the codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'Source sync: fetch/checkout the new main into the box's deploy checkout (the compose build context)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate how the auto-deploy runner acquires the Gerrit admin SSH key for setup-project.sh (e.g., store it in SSM and materialize it on the box, or confirm setup-project.sh is excluded from unattended re-runs and refs/meta/config is only applied via the existing manual operator path).
findings.finding: setup-project.sh requires a Gerrit admin SSH private key (GERRIT_ADMIN_SSH_KEY, defaulting to ~/.ssh/gerrit_admin) that exists only on an operator's workstation — it is NOT stored in SSM and is NOT available in an unattended auto-deploy context. The plan names setup-project.sh as an applier to re-run on each main advance but does not enumerate this credential dependency or explain how the auto-deploy runner acquires it.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply (idempotent, change-detected)': 'setup-project.sh (refs/meta/config)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a new SSM parameter (e.g. /rebar/prod/gerrit-admin-ssh-key) and materialize it on the box at deploy time, or (b) explicitly note that setup-project.sh requires a manual operator step and is NOT part of the auto-deploy loop (only compose-related and replication config changes auto-apply).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the Gerrit admin SSH private key (currently workstation-only, not in SSM) is made available to the unattended deploy orchestrator on the box — either store it in a new SSM SecureString slot and materialize it (mirroring deploy-key handling), or replace the SSH-dependent steps in setup-project.sh with Gerrit REST API calls using the admin password already in SSM (`/rebar/prod/gerrit-admin-password`).
findings.finding: setup-project.sh requires a Gerrit admin SSH private key (GERRIT_ADMIN_SSH_KEY, defaulting to ~/.ssh/gerrit_admin) that is documented as a workstation-held credential and is NOT stored in any SSM parameter. The plan asserts this script will be re-run idempotently by the auto-deploy orchestrator on the box, but the box has no path to acquire that key unattended — the auto-deploy will fail at the key-existence check on line 30 of setup-project.sh.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply (idempotent, change-detected): re-run … setup-project.sh (refs/meta/config)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the container image preservation step before rebuild (e.g., `docker tag review-bot:current review-bot:rollback` or capturing the prior image digest) so that the 'roll back to prior image' guarantee in the Safety section and AC4 is executable and provable.
findings.finding: The container rollback path ('roll back the container to the prior image') is stated but the mechanism for preserving the prior image before rebuild is not specified. `docker compose up -d --build` replaces the image in-place; without an explicit tag/save step beforehand, there may be no prior image to roll back to.
findings.location: What > Safety; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Before `docker compose up -d --build review-bot`, record the current image digest (`docker inspect --format='{{.Image}}' compose-review-bot-1 > .prior-image`); on failure, restore with `docker tag <prior-digest> review-bot:latest && docker compose up -d review-bot` without `--build`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-deploy image-preservation step to the deploy script design: before running `docker compose up -d --build review-bot`, tag the currently-running image (e.g., `docker tag $(docker compose images -q review-bot) review-bot:pre-deploy`) so the rollback step (`docker tag review-bot:pre-deploy ... && docker compose up -d --no-build review-bot`) has a concrete target.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback mechanism ('roll back the container to the prior image') assumes the prior image is available after a `docker compose up -d --build`, but Docker does not automatically retain the previous image under a stable tag — without an explicit pre-deploy tag step, the prior image cannot be reliably referenced.
findings.location: What section — 'Safety' bullet: 'on failure, do NOT leave a half-updated env (roll back the container to the prior image)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'within a bounded window (record the latency)' with a concrete latency SLO (e.g. '≤ 15 minutes from merge to running environment reflecting the new SHA') so AC 1 is evaluable against a pre-stated target.
findings.finding: AC 1's latency target is unbounded: 'within a bounded window (record the latency)' means any latency satisfies the criterion because the bound is set after the fact by whoever implements it, making the criterion unmeasurable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 1
findings.suggested_fix: 'A change landing on main causes the running environment to reflect it within 15 minutes (p95) of merge, with NO manual action on the box — verified by recording the merge timestamp and the deploy-log timestamp from an observed end-to-end run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff behavior for transient failures in the deploy loop (e.g., retry git fetch up to N times with exponential backoff before classifying as a hard failure and triggering rollback).
findings.finding: The deploy loop has no retry/backoff for transient failures (e.g., transient git fetch network error, Docker registry hiccup). The plan only specifies rollback on failure, meaning a single transient error immediately triggers rollback and leaves the environment on the old version until the next poll cycle, with no attempt to retry the operation.
findings.location: What / Safety bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a retry policy to the deploy script: wrap git fetch and docker compose build/up in a retry loop (e.g., up to 3 attempts, 30s backoff) before declaring failure and initiating rollback; distinguish transient vs. permanent failures in the structured log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concurrency guard to the deploy script (e.g., `flock -n /var/lock/gerrit-autodeploy.lock` or a systemd `Type=oneshot` with `ConditionPathExists` check) so that only one deploy runs at a time and concurrent triggers are safely skipped or queued.
findings.finding: No concurrency guard (advisory lock, flock, or PID file) is specified around the deploy script invocation. Under a systemd timer, if a deploy takes longer than the polling interval, a second instance will start while the first is still running; under the GHA trigger, two rapid pushes can trigger two concurrent SSM invocations.
findings.location: What section — 'Trigger' choice + Safety
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the deploy script with `flock -n /var/lock/gerrit-autodeploy.lock -c '...'` and exit 0 (skip) if the lock cannot be acquired. For the GHA path, use a `concurrency:` group key in the workflow YAML so only one deploy job runs at a time.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a hard timeout for the post-redeploy health check (e.g., 'health check must confirm liveness within 60 seconds; if it does not, treat as a failed deploy and initiate rollback').
findings.finding: The post-redeploy health check has no specified timeout. If the redeployed container hangs at startup (e.g., bot blocks waiting for a resource), the health check could block the deploy script indefinitely, preventing any subsequent deploys or timer cycles from running.
findings.location: What / Safety bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--health-timeout` and a deadline to the health check step (e.g., `timeout 60 docker compose exec review-bot curl -sf http://localhost:PORT/health || { rollback; exit 1; }`); document the timeout value in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each 'verified'/'proven' AC, add the concrete proving command or log artifact that constitutes evidence (e.g. for idempotency: 'a second run of setup-replication.sh in the same state exits 0 and produces no config diff'; for rollback: 'docker inspect compose-review-bot-1 --format {{.Image}} returns the prior SHA'; for health check: name the endpoint/command that must return 200/OK).
findings.finding: Several ACs claim outcomes are 'verified' or 'proven' without specifying a concrete proving command or observable evidence, making it impossible to confirm done-ness in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 2 ('verified for replication.config…'), AC 4 ('rollback proven'), AC 4 ('health check gates success')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the review-bot source change-detection file-set in a single configurable location (e.g., a variable at the top of the deploy script or a shared `.deploy-paths` config file) and reference it from both the deploy script and any CI path filters.
findings.finding: The file-set used for change-detection of review-bot sources (`src/rebar/**`, `Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `pyproject`) is a business rule expected to evolve (new source dirs, renamed files, added services) but appears to be embedded in deploy script logic with no mention of configurability or a single authoritative location.
findings.location: ## What — Container redeploy (change-detected)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `REVIEWBOT_SOURCE_PATHS` variable at the top of the deploy script listing the watched globs; document that adding a new source dir requires updating this variable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism for checking CI 'Verified' status before deploying (e.g. 'query GitHub Commit Status API using a stored PAT via: gh api /repos/ORG/REPO/commits/{sha}/check-runs | jq …') including the credential/auth method and the fallback behavior if the check is unreachable (fail-open vs fail-closed).
findings.finding: The mechanism for detecting CI 'Verified' status on main tip before deploying is unspecified — multiple incompatible implementation paths exist (GitHub Commit Status API, GitHub Actions check-runs API, a local git-notes convention, a custom file in the repo) and the choice affects IAM permissions, network reachability from the EC2 host, and failure semantics.
findings.location: What section — Safety bullet ('deploy ONLY a main commit whose CI Verified passed')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the custom SHA-tracking / change-detected rebuild orchestrator against Docker Compose's own layer-cache rebuild idempotency and established tools (Watchtower, `docker compose watch`) — confirm the custom approach is necessary and document why `docker compose up -d --build` alone is insufficient.
findings.finding: The plan designs a custom change-detected container-rebuild orchestrator (SHA tracking, source-diff gate, rollback) without considering established prior art: `docker compose up -d --build` already skips rebuilding layers whose inputs haven't changed (Docker layer cache), and tools like Watchtower (`containrrr/watchtower`) or `docker compose watch` (Compose v2.22+) address the 'auto-update container when source changes' use case directly. The custom approach may duplicate built-in capability or could benefit from being justified against these alternatives.
findings.location: What section — 'Container redeploy (change-detected)' and 'Source sync'
findings.suggested_fix: Consider whether `docker compose up -d --build` (already used in compose-up.sh) provides sufficient change-detection via Docker's layer cache without a separate SHA-tracking mechanism. If additional control is needed, `docker compose watch` (Compose v2.22+) or a minimal wrapper around the existing compose-up.sh is likely simpler than a custom SHA-state machine.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `setup-replication.sh` is invoked in the auto-deploy path: for a running system with the replication plugin already loaded, pass `ALLOW_REMOTE_RELOAD=1` (remote plugin admin may need enabling, or use `docker compose exec gerrit gerrit plugin reload replication` as shown in review-bot-ops.md) so the script provides a positive reload confirmation rather than printing a restart advisory — and note this in the plan's config re-apply description.
findings.finding: The plan re-runs `setup-replication.sh` as a no-restart config applier in the auto-deploy path, but the script's `ALLOW_REMOTE_RELOAD` defaults to 0, causing it to print a 'RESTART required' message and providing no programmatic confirmation that the replication plugin picked up the new config — even though `autoReload=true` in replication.config would actually handle it for a running system.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply' bullet: 'setup-replication.sh (replication.config; `autoReload=true` means no restart)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly scope Gerrit-image auto-redeploy in the plan: either (a) exclude Gerrit-image changes from auto-deploy (require manual redeploy with a maintenance window, as currently practiced) and note that only `review-bot` sources trigger automated redeploy, or (b) if Gerrit-image auto-redeploy is in scope, add a maintenance-window guard (e.g., only redeploy Gerrit during a low-traffic window, with an operator notification) and note the restart behavior.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'a Gerrit-image change likewise' triggers a rebuild but provides no specification of how a Gerrit container redeploy (which restarts Gerrit, causing a service interruption) is handled — no downtime window, no staged approach, and no mention of whether this is in-scope for the auto-deploy or requires a manual step.
findings.location: What section — 'Container redeploy' bullet: 'rebuild + restart the `review-bot` service ONLY when its sources changed... a Gerrit-image change likewise'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step to capture the auto-deploy architecture in an ADR or CLAUDE.md section (covering: trigger choice and rationale, safety invariants, rollback contract, and the manual-path escape hatch) before or alongside implementation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new deployment substrate (auto-deploy mechanism) as an architectural decision but does not plan to capture it in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc — leaving no durable record of WHY this approach was chosen, what alternatives were considered, or what the constraints/contracts are.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / AC item 5
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/auto-deploy.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section) covering: chosen trigger + why, safety invariants (Verified check, health gate, rollback), file-change detection contract, and how to disable; reference it from the deploy script header.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and update any existing deploy runbooks/READMEs that describe the manual SSM/setup-*.sh path to reflect its new role as an emergency/back-out procedure, and add a prerequisite step to disable auto-deploy before manual intervention.
findings.finding: Existing runbooks or documentation describing the manual deploy path (SSM / setup-*.sh as the canonical deploy mechanism) are not addressed for staleness — after auto-deploy lands, those docs describe a secondary/emergency path but may read as THE normal path.
findings.location: ## Why / Back-out section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC5 to explicitly require that each deploy log entry records the from→to commit SHA (or add a dedicated sub-criterion), so the Observability deliverable is fully covered.
findings.finding: The Observability deliverable specifies 'each deploy records the from→to SHA' but no AC verifies this specific requirement — AC5 covers 'deploy log + failure metric/alarm' but omits SHA recording.
findings.location: What > Observability; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC5 to: '…deploy log + failure metric/alarm applied; each deploy log entry records the from→to SHA (verified by inspecting a log entry after a test deploy).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'autoReload=true means no restart' assertion: per setup-replication.sh (lines 13–18) and ADR-0010, remote plugin admin is DISABLED so replication.config is only loaded at Gerrit startup; plan the auto-deploy to restart the Gerrit container (or set ALLOW_REMOTE_RELOAD=1 only if remote admin is re-enabled) when replication.config changes.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'autoReload=true means no restart' for setup-replication.sh re-runs, but the actual script (lines 13–18, 103–110) explicitly contradicts this: remote plugin admin is DISABLED on this instance, so replication.config is loaded only at Gerrit startup, and a restart IS required; autoReload only re-reads the file on subsequent changes after the plugin is already loaded from a prior startup.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply (idempotent, change-detected): setup-replication.sh (replication.config; autoReload=true means no restart)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan: for replication.config changes, the auto-deploy must restart the gerrit container (`docker compose restart gerrit`) after running setup-replication.sh. Alternatively, enable remote plugin admin and set ALLOW_REMOTE_RELOAD=1, but that requires a separate config change to the instance first.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's assertion that setup-replication.sh and materialize-g2p-config.sh are themselves 'change-detected': they are not; change detection must be implemented externally in the deploy orchestrator (e.g. `git diff <deployed-sha> <new-sha> -- infra/gerrit/replication.config`) before deciding whether to invoke each script.
findings.finding: The plan describes setup-replication.sh and materialize-g2p-config.sh as 'change-detected' (re-run only when the relevant file changed), but the actual scripts are not change-detected: setup-replication.sh unconditionally copies replication.config and re-runs materialize-deploy-key.sh on every invocation; materialize-g2p-config.sh unconditionally re-fetches from SSM and rewrites the ini on every run.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply (idempotent, change-detected): re-run the declarative appliers only when the relevant file changed'
findings.suggested_fix: The deploy orchestrator should use `git diff --name-only <prev_sha> <new_sha>` to detect which files changed, then conditionally invoke each setup script. This is a new wrapper layer, not something the existing scripts provide.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that setup-replication.sh's 'no restart' property holds only when replication.config is already loaded (incremental autoReload path), and specify whether the auto-deploy handles the first-apply / post-boot restart case or defers it to the existing manual operator path.
findings.finding: The plan states 'autoReload=true means no restart' for setup-replication.sh, but the script's own header explicitly documents that autoReload only handles SUBSEQUENT file changes — the FIRST load of a brand-new replication.config (or after a plugin reset) still requires a Gerrit restart, and remote plugin admin is DISABLED on this instance. The plan does not classify this first-apply restart as in-scope or enumerate how the auto-deploy handles it.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply (idempotent, change-detected)': 'setup-replication.sh (replication.config; autoReload=true means no restart)'
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow the claim: 'autoReload=true handles incremental file changes (no restart); first-apply after a fresh boot or plugin reset still requires a Gerrit restart, which the auto-deploy does NOT perform (deferred to compose-up.sh restart cycle or operator).' If auto-deploy should cover this, the script must check whether the plugin is loaded and optionally restart.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Verified-check mechanism in the 'Safety' section: since the Gerrit two-vote gate (confirmed active in `infra/gerrit/project.config`) enforces `label:Verified=MAX` before any commit can land on `main`, the deploy script can rely on gate-time enforcement as a design-time guarantee rather than a runtime poll — record this rationale explicitly so the implementer does not add a racy or redundant runtime check.
findings.finding: The plan's 'deploy ONLY a `main` commit whose CI `Verified` passed' safety gate has no specified mechanism for checking Verified status post-merge, creating either a silent no-op or a TOCTOU vulnerability depending on implementation.
findings.location: What section — 'Safety' bullet; AC line 'Safety: only a `Verified`-passing `main` tip is deployed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that 'autoReload=true means no restart' for setup-replication.sh: on this instance remote plugin admin is DISABLED (confirmed in the script), so the auto-deploy orchestrator must either restart the Gerrit container after copying replication.config, or enable remote plugin admin (and address the admin SSH key availability issue) — document the chosen approach.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'autoReload=true means no restart' for the setup-replication.sh re-run, but setup-replication.sh itself explicitly contradicts this: 'REMOTE PLUGIN ADMIN IS DISABLED on this instance, so step 3 falls through to the restart path: replication.config is loaded at Gerrit STARTUP.' Re-running setup-replication.sh in automation copies the file and then prints a manual restart instruction — it does NOT trigger a live reload, making the replication.config auto-deploy path incomplete.
findings.location: What section — 'Config re-apply … setup-replication.sh (replication.config; autoReload=true means no restart)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docker compose -f infra/compose/docker-compose.yml restart gerrit` step after setup-replication.sh in the orchestrator, conditioned on replication.config having actually changed (to avoid unnecessary restarts on no-op runs).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for verifying a main commit has Verified=+1 before deploy — name the Gerrit REST endpoint (e.g., `GET /changes/?q=commit:{SHA}&o=LABELS`), the auth credential to use (admin password from SSM, or the bot token already on the box at `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token`), and what the orchestrator does when the check fails or the commit is not found.
findings.finding: The plan's safety criterion 'only a Verified-passing main tip is deployed' names no mechanism for checking the Verified label on a specific commit from the deploy script. There is no existing script or CLI invocation in the repo that queries Gerrit's label votes for a given SHA. This is a new, unimplemented integration (Gerrit REST or SSH query) whose feasibility and auth path are unspecified.
findings.location: Safety section — 'deploy ONLY a main commit whose CI Verified passed (never deploy an unverified tip)'; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Use `curl -u bot:<GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN> 'https://<host>/a/changes/?q=commit:{SHA}&o=LABELS'` (bot token already in SSM at `/rebar/prod/gerrit-bot-token`) and check that the `Verified` label `approved` field is present in the response; block the deploy if absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `Verified` vote value vocabulary (enum: which label, which score values count as 'passed', which as 'failed', which as 'absent/pending') and co-locate the decision rule (how the deploy script queries and interprets the vote) with the safety-gate section of the plan.
findings.finding: The plan references the 'review-bot' as an LLM/agent-based merge-change reviewer (with 'merge-change review code' in src/rebar/**) but defines zero details about the agent's decision protocol, output schema, or the 'Verified' vote enum that the auto-deploy safety gate consumes — all three are used as inputs or outputs by the deploy orchestrator without being specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section / Safety bullet / AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small schema block under the Safety bullet: e.g. `Verified == +1 (CI label on the Gerrit change or GitHub Actions check 'ci' status == success) → deploy allowed; any other value or absent → skip/abort`. Reference the exact API (Gerrit REST /changes/?q=commit:SHA label:Verified=+1, or GHA check-runs endpoint) so the deploy script has a deterministic rule.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
